summary: O'Neil reflects on her dramatic weight loss following bariatric surgery, using vivid metaphors (gallons of milk, sponges) to describe the physical transformations her body is undergoing. She shares her theory that fat cells lose weight uniformly, creating increasingly spongy tissue and excess skin. Drawing on a failed diet attempt seven years earlier, she speculates about gut biome changes and their role in sustainable weight loss. Most pointedly, she pushes back against congratulations that equate thinness with beauty, arguing she preferred her fuller body aesthetically and only pursued weight loss for health reasons.
key_points: Weight loss of 43 pounds equals about 5 gallons of milk—a visceral way to conceptualize the physical reality of losing mass
Fat loss appears to happen uniformly across all fat cells, leading to 'spongy' tissue and significant excess skin
Gut microbiome changes may be a key mechanism behind why bariatric surgery succeeds where willpower-based diets fail
Society's conflation of thinness with beauty is harmful—O'Neil explicitly states she found her larger body more attractive
Sustainable weight loss requires psychological adjustment to bodily changes, not just dietary discipline
money_quotes: You should look at yourself as a container – most likely a bag – of liquid, he said, much like a plastic container of milk.
My skin is excessive for my needs, if you catch my drift. And that's not going away.
It was delicious, my friends, and it broke me. Somehow I could never look back.
Some people do fight every minute of every day to stay thin, but I wouldn't want to be one of them. Too many other things to do.
Please don't equate thin with beautiful. I liked my body so much more when I was fat, when I looked like an enormous buddha, full and round.
I didn't do this to look better, I did it to be healthier, to bike in the summer and to avoid diabetes.
I'm hoping to eventually find a way to love my body in spite of how it looks, but it's a monumental challenge.
key_insight: Weight loss is not a triumph of beauty but a complicated negotiation between health, identity, and a society that wrongly equates thinness with worth.
